verses.text: Asaph laments that the heathen have invaded God's land, defiled His temple, left the dead unburied, and made Israel a reproach. The desolation is total. The triumph of the ungodly over God's people is a bitter grief.

Asaph laments that the heathen have invaded God's land, defiled His temple, left the dead unburied, and made Israel a reproach. The desolation is total. The triumph of the ungodly over God's people is a bitter grief.

Asaph laments that the heathen have invaded God's land, defiled His temple, left the dead unburied, and made Israel a reproach. The desolation is total. The triumph of the ungodly over God's people is a bitter grief.

Asaph laments that the heathen have invaded God's land, defiled His temple, left the dead unburied, and made Israel a reproach. The desolation is total. The triumph of the ungodly over God's people is a bitter grief.
'How long, LORD?' he cries, asking God to pour His wrath on the nations that devoured Jacob and knew Him not. He appeals to God's justice. The wronged people commit their vengeance to God.
'How long, LORD?' he cries, asking God to pour His wrath on the nations that devoured Jacob and knew Him not. He appeals to God's justice. The wronged people commit their vengeance to God.
'How long, LORD?' he cries, asking God to pour His wrath on the nations that devoured Jacob and knew Him not. He appeals to God's justice. The wronged people commit their vengeance to God.

He pleads that God remember not former sins but show speedy mercy, and help them 'for the glory of thy name.' He rests on God's honour, not their merit. The surest plea is God's own glory in saving His people.

He pleads that God remember not former sins but show speedy mercy, and help them 'for the glory of thy name.' He rests on God's honour, not their merit. The surest plea is God's own glory in saving His people.
Why should the heathen say, 'Where is their God?' Let the prisoner's groaning come before Him. God's name is at stake. His people's deliverance answers the scoffer's taunt.
Why should the heathen say, 'Where is their God?' Let the prisoner's groaning come before Him. God's name is at stake. His people's deliverance answers the scoffer's taunt.
He asks God to repay their neighbours' reproach, that His flock may give thanks for ever. Mercy will end in praise. Delivered, the people of God will declare His faithfulness to every generation.
